skill.name: azure-eventhub-java
skill.description: Guide Java developers through Azure Event Hubs producers, consumers, processors, batching, partitioning, checkpointing, and cleanup patterns.
skill.summary: Build Azure Event Hubs streaming applications with Java SDK examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, weak-crypto keywords, and system reconnaissance keywords. Manual review found only documentation, Java examples, XML dependencies, placeholder environment variables, and no prompt injection or executable skill code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged external command locations are Markdown fences and Java, XML, or Bash examples. No Ruby shell backtick execution or command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation fences and SDK examples, not executable skill code. No command string, process launch, or shell execution API appears in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Keyword
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines contain prose and a best-practice list item. No weak cryptographic algorithm, crypto API, or hashing operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both lines were manually inspected and contain Azure Event Hubs descriptive text. The file has no cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Keyword
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged line is a checkpointing best practice for Event Hubs processing. It does not perform host, user, network, or environment reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain documentation text with no command, script, or runtime behavior. No nearby content asks the model to inspect the local system.

content.user_title: Build Azure Event Hubs Java Streams
content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable patterns for sending, receiving, and processing Azure Event Hubs data. This skill provides focused SDK examples for producers, consumers, batching, partitioning, checkpointing, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependencies for Event Hubs and Blob checkpoint storage.
Explains producer, consumer, async producer, and async consumer client setup.
Demonstrates single event sends, batch sends, partition IDs, and partition keys.
Provides EventProcessorClient patterns for checkpointed production consumers.
Covers event positions, partition properties, error handling, and resource cleanup.
limitations: Does not provision Azure resources or configure access policies.
Uses placeholder connection strings and names that users must replace.
Does not include a complete runnable project or automated tests.
Does not cover every Azure identity, networking, or monitoring option.

use_cases.title: Create a Java Event Producer
use_cases.description: Generate a producer pattern that sends individual events or batches to Azure Event Hubs.

use_cases.title: Build a Checkpointed Consumer
use_cases.description: Use EventProcessorClient with Blob checkpoint storage for balanced partition processing.

use_cases.title: Review Streaming Integration Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare partition keys, event positions, async clients, and cleanup practices before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Producer
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Java Azure Event Hubs producer using azure-messaging-eventhubs. Include dependency guidance, client creation, one event send, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Add Batching
prompt_templates.prompt: Update my Java Event Hubs producer to send events in EventDataBatch objects. Explain how to handle a full batch.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Processor
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an EventProcessorClient consumer with Blob checkpoint storage, error handling, and safe checkpoint timing for a Java service.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Partition Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Event Hubs Java design for partition keys, event positions, async clients, retry behavior, and resource cleanup risks.

output_examples.input: I need to publish order events from a Java service.
output_examples.output: A concise producer plan with required Maven packages, credential choices, batch creation, send flow, and client cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: I need a reliable consumer for several Event Hubs partitions.
output_examples.output: A processor design that uses Blob checkpoint storage, partition-aware processing, transient error handling, and checkpoint guidance.

output_examples.input: I am unsure where to start consuming from.
output_examples.output: A comparison of earliest, latest, offset, sequence number, and enqueue time positions with practical selection guidance.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential when managed identity or developer credentials are available.
Use EventDataBatch for high-throughput publishing and handle full batches explicitly.
Checkpoint after successful processing and close clients during shutdown.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode production connection strings in source code.
Do not checkpoint before downstream processing succeeds.
Do not send unordered related events with random partition keys.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure Event Hubs resources?
faq.answer: No. It guides Java SDK usage after the namespace, event hub, and storage resources exist.

faq.question: Can it use Microsoft Entra authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes DefaultAzureCredential setup for Event Hubs clients.

faq.question: Does it support async Java clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes producer and consumer async client examples and reactive receive guidance.

faq.question: Is checkpoint storage covered?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows Blob checkpoint store usage with EventProcessorClient for production consumers.

faq.question: Does it include secrets management?
faq.answer: Only at a basic level. It uses placeholders and recommends avoiding hardcoded production secrets.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
